data.title: O-I Glass outlines 2026 adjusted EPS of $1.00-$1.50 as it targets at least $275M of Fit to Win benefits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: O-I Glass (OI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Gordon Hardie said Q1 started “challenging,” with “demand… sluggish early in the quarter before improving through March,” and added that “first quarter adjusted earnings of $0.05 per share came in below our original expectations.” He highlighted pressure in Europe from “elevated commercial pressures… and several onetime external events that increased our costs,” while reiterating, “our strategy and priorities are unchanged.”
  • Hardie framed a sharper regional split: “In the Americas, earnings were stable,” while “in Europe, results fell short of expectations amid elevated competitive pressure,” adding that Europe is “earlier in the Fit to Win journey than the Americas.”
  • Hardie pointed to improving demand cadence and new wins: shipments were “down about 8% versus the prior year,” but “volume trends improved sequentially through the quarter, with March volumes down only 2%,” and the company has “15 confirmed incremental volume wins in hand… that should contribute 1.5% of new sales volume starting in the second half of the year.”
  • CFO John Haudrich reported: “First quarter net sales were $1.54 billion, essentially flat with the prior year,” and said results reflected “commercial headwinds, including unfavorable net price and lower volumes,” plus “an unusually high effective tax rate on low pretax earnings.”

Outlook

  • Haudrich said the company “updated our full year guidance to adjusted earnings of $1 to $1.50 per share,” citing Europe where the company “risk-adjusted our outlook by up to $25 million given elevated competitive pressures.”
  • Haudrich identified energy as the key variable: “macro-driven energy inflation… could total $75 million to $100 million,” while adding that “approximately 75% to 80% of gas requirements are protected” in Europe.
  • Hardie reiterated the volume setup: “we continue to expect full year sales volumes to be about flat with the prior year,” with shipments “stable in the second quarter” and “low to mid-single-digit growth in the second half.”

Financial Results

  • Haudrich said segment operating profit was “$142 million, down from $209 million last year,” driven by “commercial pressures.”
  • In the Americas, Haudrich said segment operating profit was “$142 million, essentially flat year-over-year,” and noted “$10 million of disruption-related expense driven by extreme weather, civil unrest in Mexico and a natural gas pipeline failure in Peru.”
  • In Europe, Haudrich said “profitability compressed meaningfully,” with segment operating profit “breakeven,” and attributed the decline primarily to “a $76 million reduction in net price,” also reflecting “the reset of favorable energy contracts that expired last year.”

Q&A

  • George Staphos, BofA Securities: asked why management and the Board were confident “the turn is happening in 2Q” and probed energy sensitivity and covenants; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said the guidance assumes “EUR 45 to EUR 55 per megawatt hour,” adding “for every EUR 5 drop on average, we get back about $0.05 per share,” and stated, “we’re not anywhere near at risk” on secured debt covenants, citing “$1.5 billion of liquidity.”
  • Michael Roxland, Truist: asked for a split of the “15 accounts” wins and end-market sources; CEO Hardie: said the 1.5% annualized volume is “split about 70%, 75% Americas, 25%, 30% Europe,” and added, “in North America, for the first time, we’re starting to make inroads into RTDs.”
  • Roxland, Truist: asked how the company still reaches the 2027 target after an implied 2026 setback; CEO Hardie: said the company remains “laser-focused on our 2027 Investor Day targets… $1.45 billion,” citing “$150 million of Fit to Win to come in 2027” and “price adjustment formulas that are lagged.”
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: asked about Southern Europe oversupply dynamics and share shifts; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said Europe capacity utilization is improving with “announced capacity closures underway,” and argued this can support “a much more supply-demand balance.”
  • Gaurav Sharma, UBS: asked for optimal European utilization and whether more sites could be idled; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said “something in the 90s, low 90s is a great place to be for a glass plant,” and described prior “13%, 14% excess capacity” reducing to “low single digits.”
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: pressed whether new wins can offset market declines and if consistent 1%-2% growth is achievable; CEO Hardie: said volumes “have been below what we thought,” but added, “we really feel we’ve bottomed out,” and described year-end volume expectations as “net.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated pressure on the Europe “breakeven” outcome, the “steep climb” to 2027 targets, and questions on energy, utilization, and covenants.
  • Management: slightly positive in forward framing but defensive on Europe and energy, emphasizing control levers and hedging; Haudrich said, “we’re pretty confident” in the energy exposure range and Hardie said, “we are not satisfied with our first quarter results, and we are moving quickly to improve performance.”
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the tone shifted from “positive momentum” and an improving 2026 setup to a more cautious posture with an “updated… 2026 guidance” and heavier focus on Europe pricing and energy inflation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language softened: Q4 2025 projected 2026 adjusted EPS of “$1.65 to $1.90,” while Q1 2026 revised to “$1 to $1.50 per share.”
  • The earnings bridge shifted from Q4’s emphasis on Fit to Win momentum and an energy “step-up” to Q1’s emphasis on Europe net price pressure, “breakeven” profitability in Europe, and a larger near-term macro-energy swing factor.
  • Analysts pivoted from Q4 questions on volume cadence and cash flow levers to Q1 questions centered on Europe competitive intensity, energy sensitivities, and downside protection.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hardie said Europe faced “elevated competitive pressure” and Haudrich tied Europe’s Q1 compression to a “$76 million reduction in net price,” alongside low utilization, “most notably in wine in Southern Europe.”
  • Haudrich flagged uncertainty from “conflicts in the Middle East” driving “$75 million to $100 million” in energy inflation, while describing mitigation via hedging and contractual structures: “More than half of our business operates under contractual price adjustment formulas.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a below-expectations start driven by Europe pricing pressure and external cost events, while keeping the strategy centered on Fit to Win execution, network actions in Europe, and a new go-to-market push tied to 15 incremental account wins. The company revised 2026 adjusted EPS to $1-$1.50, framed energy inflation and European competitiveness as the main swing factors, and emphasized liquidity, hedging coverage, and lagged pricing mechanisms as key supports as it targets stronger second-half volume and momentum into 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: InvenTrust raises 2026 NAREIT FFO guidance to $2.00-$2.06 per share while targeting $300M net investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: InvenTrust Properties Corp. (IVT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Daniel Busch): "Our first quarter results reflected steady operating performance across the portfolio. Same-property NOI grew 2.6%, while core FFO and NAREIT FFO per share increased 6.5% and 10.4%, respectively, from the first quarter of 2025."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "Given this visibility, coupled with increased confidence around our acquisition pipeline, we were able to increase FFO per share guidance for 2026."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "During the quarter, we completed $123 million towards our $300 million net investment guidance for the year, and we have another $167 million of additional deals awarded or under contract with other opportunities still in the pipeline."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Michael Phillips): "Subsequent to quarter end in April, we signed a definitive note purchase agreement for a $250 million private placement of senior unsecured notes."
  • Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary Christy David): "We executed 64 leases covering approximately 329,000 square feet and comparable blended spreads were 10.5%, with new leases at 19.8% and renewals at 9.9%."

Outlook

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We are reaffirming our full year same-property NOI growth guidance range of 3.25% to 4.25%."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "For NAREIT FFO, we are increasing our full year guidance range to $2 to $2.06 per share, which represents a 7.4% growth at the midpoint versus 2025."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Our core FFO guidance is increasing to $1.92 to $1.96 per share, up 6% at the midpoint from last year."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "This increase is primarily driven by mark-to-market lease adjustments related to our recent acquisitions."

Financial results

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Same-property NOI for the quarter totaled $48.7 million, an increase of 2.6% over the first quarter of 2025."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "NAREIT FFO for the quarter totaled $41.3 million or $0.53 per diluted share, reflecting a 10.4% increase from the first quarter of 2025. Core FFO rose 6.5% to $0.49 per share year-over-year."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We also recognized approximately $800,000 of lease termination fee income during the quarter, which was anticipated and incorporated into our initial guidance."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "At quarter end, total liquidity stood at $346 million, including $27 million of cash and $319 million available on our revolving credit facility."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Net leverage finished the quarter at 29.7% and net debt to adjusted EBITDA was 5.2x on a trailing 12-month basis."

Q&A

  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "Are those expected to close by roughly the end of the second quarter?" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "I think we're hoping that most of those will close at some point in the second quarter... maybe leaking a little bit into the third quarter."
  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "How should we think about equity capital sort of fitting into the equation" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "But having said that, it all is predicated on the opportunity set. And if the opportunity set is one where we can continue to grow cash flow accretively, we'll look at all different avenues."
  • Andrew Reale, BofA Securities, Research Division: "Do you have any additional Nashville assets in the pipeline currently?" Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "We do have a few other assets in the pipeline. Nothing currently under LOI or near execution."
  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: "I wanted to ask about the same-property NOI acceleration in the back half of the year" Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We do expect 90% of that to be coming online by the end of the year... in Q3 and Q4 is when you'll see most of that come online."
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG, LLC, Research Division: "for those 7 larger format small shop tenants, was there anything thematic in there?" Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "there's nothing systematic or thematic about what departed in that area."
  • Paulina Rojas Schmidt, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: "have you seen any shift in terms of cap rates?" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "I think it's much of the same as opposed to any material difference from maybe last quarter or even a couple of quarters ago."

Sentiment analysis

  • Quote (Paulina Rojas Schmidt, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division): "have you seen any shift in terms of cap rates?" and Quote (Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division): "I wanted to ask about the same-property NOI acceleration" reflected a neutral-to-slightly skeptical tone focused on pricing, cadence, and conversion, while repeatedly pressing for timing and durability.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "we feel pretty good about our multiple. We feel good about where the stock is at" and Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "If we didn't have the demand right behind those, perhaps I would tell you that there would be some softness, but that's absolutely not the case" reflected slightly positive, confidence-forward framing in both prepared remarks and Q&A.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q4 2025: "The competition is still very strong" versus Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q1 2026: "Not really, to be frank" (on distress) suggested a similar competitive backdrop, with Q1 commentary leaning more toward execution visibility and pipeline depth.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q4 2025: "For NAREIT FFO, we are providing guidance in the range of $1.97 to $2.03 per share" versus Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q1 2026: "we are increasing our full year guidance range to $2 to $2.06 per share" highlighted a higher full-year range while keeping same-property NOI growth guidance unchanged.
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q4 2025: "At year-end, total liquidity stood at $480 million" versus Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q1 2026: "total liquidity stood at $346 million" alongside Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "a $250 million private placement" emphasized a shift toward terming out capital as acquisition activity progressed.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q4 2025: "almost half of that $300 million is either -- has either been awarded or is under contract" versus Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q1 2026: "we completed $123 million" and "another $167 million" pointed to greater year-to-date conversion and a larger identified near-term closing set.

Risks and concerns

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "These gains were partially offset by a 40 basis point headwind from bad debt and 60 basis points from an expected temporary impact in occupancy" framed near-term operational offsets to NOI growth.
  • Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "Painted Tree Marketplace closed stores across the U.S., including our one location in Glen Allen, Virginia" and "representing approximately 30,000 square feet or about 20 basis points of ABR" identified a tenant-specific vacancy risk, with mitigation in Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "We are well positioned to backfill this space."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "It continues -- like we're continuing to find really good opportunities, but there hasn't been a whole lot of distress on the seller side" underscored a competitive acquisition environment without distressed supply.

Final takeaway

Management described steady operating performance and raised 2026 FFO per-share guidance while reaffirming same-property NOI growth expectations, pointing to back-half NOI acceleration from signed-not-open commencements and continued embedded rent growth. Executives emphasized acquisition momentum toward the $300 million net investment plan, alongside a $250 million senior unsecured notes private placement intended to support capital deployment, while acknowledging temporary occupancy impact and specific tenant move-outs as near-term items they expect to re-lease at higher prospective rents.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scotts Miracle-Gro targets 2030 $1B incremental sales and plans share buyback of at least 1/3 of shares
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company (SMG) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Through our first 6 months of the fiscal year, we continued on our growth trajectory and made progress toward every single one of our full year financial imperatives." (CEO & Chairman of the Board James Hagedorn) "They include closing the quarter with leverage at 3.71x debt-to-EBITDA... and the divestiture of Hawthorne." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're ready to embark on the first tranche of the multiyear share repurchase program we announced last quarter and said would begin once leverage was comfortably in the 3s." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "The ultimate goal is to buy back at least 1/3 of our outstanding shares." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "The 2030 target of an incremental $1 billion in top line sales, a gross margin rate approaching 40% and total EBITDA north of $1 billion." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "We think upwards of $800 million of top line sales growth under SMG 2.0 will be generated through e-commerce alone." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're announcing the hiring of a Chief Brand Officer... The person we've selected has agreed to start in June." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "With Chris Hagedorn coming off Hawthorne, he'll be able to devote more time to our core business... Chris will lead company strategy with focus on business development." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "So far in fiscal '26, we've introduced 83 new product SKUs, accounting for $41 million in revenue." (President & COO Nate Baxter) "We have line of sight to eliminate 30% of our lowest-performing SKUs by next fiscal year." (President & COO Baxter)

  • "In the second quarter, total company net sales increased 5% to $1.46 billion." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Mark Scheiwer) "E-commerce POS trends continue to demonstrate the effectiveness of our channel expansion... Year-to-date e-comm POS dollars were up 22%." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Outlook

  • "As we look to the rest of the year, we're reaffirming our guidance and will not let commodities steer us off course despite global supply pressures from the Iran war." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "Fiscal '27 is a bigger unknown... we will... take pricing in fiscal '27 if necessary. We will not sacrifice our gross margin goals." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're tracking to our targets for net sales growth, gross margin expansion and leverage reduction." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "We are reaffirming our fiscal '26 guidance and have a high degree of optimism for the long-term financial goals." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "Urea specifically, we have flexibility... we're going to delay purchases a bit this year relative to how we've done it in the past." (President & COO Baxter) "We're committed to taking pricing if we have to." (President & COO Baxter)

Financial Results

  • "In the quarter, the GAAP and non-GAAP gross margin rate was 41.8%, a 280 basis point improvement and a 240 basis point improvement, respectively, over prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "SG&A in the quarter increased 12%... The increase... was expected and reflects our increased media and marketing spend." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "For the quarter, GAAP net income from continuing operations was $263.3 million or $4.46 per share." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "Adjusted non-GAAP net income... was $267.8 million or $4.53 per share." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "We leverage at 3.71x... was a result of higher EBITDA and continued deployment of free cash flow to debt reduction." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "Year-to-date, free cash flow was favorable by more than $100 million over prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Q&A

  • Jon Andersen, William Blair: "Could you talk a little bit about... the restage on the lawns business... and... e-com..."; President & COO Baxter: "Shipments remain strong... not concerned about inventory levels." John Sass: "We're... selling a 4-step type of solution... We're seeing another sell-through of our Halts... over 20%." Marketing Director Mike Davitt: "Consumers are moving to specifics... we're doing it in dot-com first."

  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked if SMG 2.0 build is "linear" and timing of margin actions; President & COO Baxter: "I would just look at it as linear. I don't think it will play out that way." CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Just getting our share online up... will give Nate most of what he needs to get that $1 billion."

  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked why reiterate gross margin outlook; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "We took the marketing money and said, if you want the marketing money, you're going to put it behind branded, and they did." Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "We definitely feel like we can deliver on the 32% gross margin guide." (Note: the $32% figure was stated in Q&A as "32% gross margin guide" with no additional numeric update.)

  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: asked on raw material lock timing; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "Generally... what you see in our P&L is stuff that was purchased most likely 6 to 9 months previously." President & COO Baxter: "We're going to delay purchases a bit this year."

  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: asked on RONA shop-in-shops; President & COO Baxter: "I think it's a little early to really quantify the results from that... we'll have more data" by August.

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: asked if pricing is "manageable" next year; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Yes, I absolutely feel." President & COO Baxter: "We've managed through it... I'm keeping my eye on the long ball."

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: asked e-commerce margin/work cap impact; President & COO Baxter: "There is a margin delta... It's a few hundred basis points delta." Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "We're not going to be doing direct-to-consumer... we leverage our customers." CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Not participating is suicide for us."

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: pressed on when '27 cost/pricing decisions must be made; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "You're probably talking 8 to 12 weeks where these decisions need to be made." President & COO Baxter: "Q3... that's exactly when we have to make all these decisions."

  • William Reuter, BofA: asked if extra pricing was taken; President & COO Baxter: "We haven't taken any additional pricing since establishing with the retailers last quarter."

  • William Reuter, BofA: asked leverage posture with buybacks; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Yes." "Mark has knock it off rights here." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on commodities/pricing and margin durability, including "At what point is it that you have to kind of make decisions" and whether guidance was "simply conservatism." (Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo; Peter Grom, UBS)

  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks and more forceful in Q&A on pricing and e-commerce: "We will not sacrifice our gross margin goals." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "Not participating is suicide for us." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Versus last quarter, management’s Q2 language was more execution- and capital-return-focused ("We're there" on leverage; "move forward with the repurchases") after Hawthorne divestiture closed, while analyst questions shifted from early-season phasing to '27 commodity decision timing. (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q2, management said Hawthorne divestiture was completed ("completing its divestiture in early April"), compared with Q1 describing a pending sale and initial discontinued-operations impact. (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Q2 introduced SMG 2.0 details and operating building blocks ("83 new product SKUs"; "eliminate 30% of our lowest-performing SKUs"; "about 40 use cases of AI"), while Q1 emphasized the new long-term targets and the initial repurchase authorization. (President & COO Baxter; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Q2 moved buybacks from authorization/timing to activation tied to leverage: Q1 said the $500 million program "will begin later in '26" and "begin in late 2026"; Q2 said "we're ready to embark on the first tranche" now that leverage is "comfortably in the 3s." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Global supply pressures from the Iran war" and commodity volatility were framed as a fiscal '27 uncertainty, with mitigation via hedging, contingency plans, sourcing flexibility, and pricing: "Most of our cost of goods sold are locked" for fiscal '26, and "we will... take pricing" if needed for fiscal '27. (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer; President & COO Baxter)

  • E-commerce profit structure remains a stated challenge: "There is a margin delta... It's a few hundred basis points delta," with management pointing to product innovation and leveraging retail partners rather than building a direct-to-consumer network. (President & COO Baxter; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as the completion of key balance-sheet and portfolio milestones—"leverage at 3.71x" and the Hawthorne divestiture—while shifting toward capital returns and SMG 2.0 execution, including initiating the first tranche of a multiyear repurchase program targeting "at least 1/3" of shares, and pursuing a 2030 plan for "$1 billion" incremental sales with e-commerce positioned to generate "upwards of $800 million" of that growth, alongside an explicit stance that fiscal '27 commodity inflation will be managed with hedging, sourcing actions, and pricing to avoid sacrificing gross margin goals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street inches lower after Fed decision and now looks ahead to Big Tech earnings
data.description:
Wall Street trades lower on Wednesday as investors look ahead to big tech earnings and markets digest the latest Federal Reserve rate decision.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was -0.7%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.3%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, nine of the 11 S&P segments were in the red, with materials at the bottom of the list. At the other end of the spectrum, energy was on top as oil (CL1:COM)  pushed back above $100 per barrel. 

U.S. Treasury yields topped 1-month highs. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 6 basis points to 4.41%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) ticked up 8 basis points to 3.93%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) added 4 basis points to 4.98%. 

The Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting, citing the uncertainty over the U.S. economic and global macro outlooks.

Elsewhere on the economic calendar, durable goods rebounded more than expected in March. Housing starts rose in March, while building permits fell. The U.S. international trade in goods deficit widened in March, and the wholesale and retail inventories accelerated M/M in March.

Big tech earnings data will continue to be in focus as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) will release their results after the bell. 

“Stocks are again trading at record highs, reflecting strong investor confidence, but the S&P 500's heavy concentration in the Mag 7 technology leaders elevates downside risk should earnings fall short, as valuations leave little margin for error. For now, current levels are supported by solid forward growth expectations, but that justification could quickly erode if margin compression emerges, inflation weighs on growth, or earnings signal a broader slowdown,” Chris Brigati, chief investment officer of SWBC, stated.

data.title: Jerome Powell to stay on Fed board for a time after chair term ends
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said he'll continue to serve as governor for a time after his chair term ends in May, he said on Wednesday at his post-decision press conference.

"I will leave when it's appropriate to do so," he said.

Developing… Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Seacoast projects 2026 EPS of $2.48 to $2.52 while reiterating 53% to 55% efficiency ratio target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seacoast Banking Corporation of Florida (SBCF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Charles Shaffer said the quarter was “highlighted by robust deposit growth, particularly in noninterest-bearing deposits, meaningful expansion in the net interest margin, and solid progress towards the financial guidance we introduced last quarter,” while noting Q1 loan growth was “seasonally softer and further impacted by elevated payoffs” but the pipeline “remains strong.”
  • Shaffer highlighted fee businesses and the Villages expansion, saying, “Noninterest income continued to perform well, driven by strength across wealth management, insurance, treasury and our mortgage businesses,” and added, “our expansion in the villages is already delivering results with solid mortgage production and growing demand for wealth management services.”
  • CFO Tracey Dexter framed reported vs. adjusted profitability and the securities action: “Seacoast reported net income of $31.9 million, or $0.29 per share in the first quarter,” and “Reported results include a $39.5 million pretax loss related to the strategic repositioning of a portion of our available-for-sale securities portfolio, which we executed in January.”
  • Dexter emphasized funding and margin drivers, saying, “Expansion in the net interest margin was a highlight this quarter, driven largely by lower deposit costs and the bond portfolio restructure,” and added that the company “were more active in share repurchases, buying back over 317,000 shares.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated full-year adjusted EPS guidance, with CEO Charles Shaffer stating, “we continue to expect full year earnings per share in a range of $2.48 to $2.52 despite two less rate cuts.”
  • CFO Tracey Dexter said, “On Slide 17, we reiterate the guidance we provided last quarter,” adding, “The adjusted earnings per share outlook remains unchanged at $2.48 to $2.52, with the potential for slightly lower revenue resulting from the change in previously expected rate cuts, but with no change to bottom line results.”
  • In Q&A, management tied expense and integration cadence to the efficiency outlook. Chief Strategy Officer Michael Young said the company expects “an expense step-up here in the second quarter with our normal annual pay cycle,” and “we'll see the cost out come in the back half of the year,” while CEO Shaffer pointed back to guidance: “We think a full year efficiency ratio is somewhere between 53% and 55%.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Tracey Dexter said, “On an adjusted basis, net income was $67.8 million, or $0.62 per share, increasing 42% from the prior quarter, and 111% year-over-year,” and attributed the improvement to “expanding net interest income, disciplined balance sheet management and continued execution on organic growth initiatives.”
  • Dexter reported net interest income of $178.2 million and said “The net interest margin expanded 17 basis points to 3.83%,” while adding that “excluding the impact of accretion on acquired loans, margin expanded 13 basis points to 3.57%.”
  • Dexter described fee lines and the securities-loss impact on reported noninterest income: “Reported noninterest income was a net loss of $12.6 million,” while “Adjusted noninterest income, which excludes the securities repositioning, totaled $26.9 million.”
  • On balance sheet growth and credit, Dexter said “Total deposits increased $382 million during the quarter,” and that nonperforming loans increased “to 0.75% of total loans,” while stating the move to nonaccrual for two credits had “collateral values well in excess of balances outstanding and therefore, no credit loss is expected.”

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc.: asked how Q2 pipeline looks amid uncertainty and how much Q1 deposit strength is seasonal; Chairman, President & CEO Shaffer responded that Q1 had “three larger credits pay off in aggregate, $150 million,” but “the pipeline remains strong” and the company expects to “return to high single digits,” while Chief Strategy Officer Young said seasonal tax outflows are normal but management expects to “hold higher levels of noninterest-bearing deposits as we move forward.”
  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.: asked about margin trajectory, deposit-cost direction without Fed cuts, and how efficiency flexes with revenue; Chief Strategy Officer Young said, “we do expect continued margin expansion here in the second and third quarter,” and noted, “we revised the revenue guidance low end down by 1 percentage point,” while CEO Shaffer said if revenue is higher “that does fall to the bottom line” and the company may “tighten a little bit on the expense side” while remaining “100% confident in delivering the EPS range.”
  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James & Associates: asked where growth is coming from and whether wealth balances can grow into Q2; CEO Shaffer said growth is “broad-based” with momentum in “The Villages and the expansionary markets,” and added on wealth, “we expect to continue to grow throughout the year,” citing “almost $17 million of new AUM coming out of The Villages.”
  • Kyle Gierman, Hovde Group: asked on banker hiring progress and M&A appetite; CEO Shaffer said on banker additions, “We're about halfway there,” and on M&A, “At the moment, it's heads down, focused on integration,” adding the company remains “focused only on Florida from an M&A perspective.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on payoffs, macro uncertainty, deposit-cost direction without Fed cuts, and the Villages cost-save cadence, including questions framed around “macro uncertainty,” “Fed on pause,” and expense “glide path.”
  • Management: slightly positive, leaning confident on guidance and integration execution, including CEO Shaffer’s “remain confident in the guidance,” “100% confident in delivering the EPS range,” and “we're going to deliver a flawless conversion.”
  • Versus last quarter: the tone stayed confident on the same EPS range, but the current call added more emphasis on rate-cut expectations and how that changes revenue assumptions, with Young explicitly stating, “we revised the revenue guidance low end down by 1 percentage point,” while maintaining the same bottom-line EPS guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance messaging remained anchored to the same adjusted EPS range of $2.48 to $2.52, with Q1 emphasizing that it holds “despite two less rate cuts,” compared with Q4’s framing around exiting 2026 post-conversion with profitability targets.
  • Strategic emphasis moved from Q4’s acquisition close and securities-portfolio actions to Q1’s execution phase, with Q1 highlighting January’s AFS repositioning loss and reinvestment, and management repeatedly focusing on the summer Villages conversion timeline.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s deeper questions on purchase accounting and additional securities restructures to Q1’s questions on payoffs, pipeline durability, deposit mix, expense cadence, and conversion-linked cost saves.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged elevated payoffs as a near-term headwind, with CEO Charles Shaffer saying, “payoffs were very elevated,” while mitigating with “the pipeline remains strong” and expectations that “payoffs [will] moderate in the coming quarters.”
  • The company tied part of its revenue sensitivity to fewer rate cuts, with Chief Strategy Officer Michael Young stating, “Without Fed cuts... we could see some stabilizing to increasing deposit costs potentially later this year.”
  • Integration and execution risk around the Villages conversion was a prominent theme, with CEO Shaffer stating, “We want to get this absolutely 100% right,” and “we're going to deliver a flawless conversion.”

Final Takeaway

Seacoast’s management positioned Q1 as progress toward the guidance introduced last quarter, emphasizing deposit-led funding improvement and margin expansion while holding the full-year adjusted EPS range of $2.48 to $2.52 even as the company adjusted revenue assumptions for fewer expected rate cuts. Executives underscored disciplined expenses alongside a conversion-driven cost-save cadence, with the Villages integration described as the near-term priority, while payoffs and the rate environment were identified as key variables that could influence loan growth and deposit-cost trends through 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Orion outlines $24B pipeline while reaffirming full-year 2026 guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Orion Group Holdings (ORN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered a solid start to the year, supported by disciplined operational performance and a healthy $24 billion pipeline of opportunities,” said CEO Travis Boone, adding, “This translated into top and bottom line growth and good cash flow generation.” (CEO, President & Director Travis Boone)
  • “At the end of the quarter, backlog stood at $668 million and included almost $220 million in new awards and change orders booked in the quarter,” Boone said, and added, “We’ve continued the bookings momentum into April and have been awarded well over $200 million in new work that is not yet under contract, so it is not in our backlog.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • “Turning to Concrete. This team delivered a fantastic quarter across all key metrics,” Boone said, including, “In the quarter, data centers accounted for around 40% of concrete revenues.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • “We’re pleased to report first quarter revenue of $216 million, GAAP net income of $4.7 million, adjusted EBITDA of $8.7 million and adjusted EPS of $0.05 per share,” said CFO Alison Vasquez, and added, “We have revised our presentation to begin reporting 3 segments: Marine, Concrete and Corporate.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Alison Vasquez)
  • “Tom Amonett and Peggy Foran… will be retiring from our Board at the annual meeting, at which time the size of our Board will decrease from 8 directors to 6 directors,” Boone said. (CEO, President & Director Boone)

Outlook

  • “With growing backlog and a robust pipeline, we are pleased to reaffirm our full year 2026 guidance,” Boone said. (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • On keeping guidance unchanged, Vasquez said, “We just initiated the guidance last month,” and added, “the results came in pretty much right in line with what we expected from a profitability perspective.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)
  • Boone reinforced a conservative posture on guidance: “We generally, we want to underpromise and overdeliver… we’re going to hold the line for now and see how things progress over the next quarter or two.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)

Financial Results

  • Vasquez said the company reported Q1 revenue of $216 million, GAAP net income of $4.7 million, adjusted EBITDA of $8.7 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.05. (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)
  • Segment detail included: “Our Marine segment reported revenue of $110 million and adjusted EBITDA of $12 million, representing an 11% margin,” and “Our Concrete business… reporting revenue of $106 million and adjusted EBITDA of $8.6 million, representing an 8% margin.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)
  • On leverage and acquisition funding, Vasquez said, “we ended the quarter with just over $70 million of debt… which we used to fund the McAmis acquisition in the quarter,” and added, “Our net leverage remains at a healthy level.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)

Q&A

  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked why guidance was not raised despite Q1 and April updates; CFO Vasquez responded, “it was prudent just to kind of hold where we are,” and CEO Boone added, “we want to underpromise and overdeliver.”
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked about margin recovery after Q2 and Marine margin contraction; Vasquez said the margin impact was “attributable to just phasing” and “more of a timing item,” adding, “We don’t see… any consistent or persistent margin degradation over time.”
  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: Asked about timing from early energy/petrochem work to awards and potential size; Vasquez said timelines could be “3 months, 6 months or a year,” and added that projects more advanced in permitting “would expect those to move forward more quickly.”
  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: Asked about defense-budget-related marine opportunities; Boone said the President’s budget “is a bit of a wish list that still has to get put in place by Congress,” while Vasquez said even “if it goes up to $1 trillion, that’s still a very large increase.”
  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: Asked about fuel, tariffs, and Section 232 impacts; Boone said, “we tend to build in contingency in our bids,” adding, “We’re generally in pretty good shape on the tariff side of things.”
  • Min Cho, Texas Capital: Asked if Concrete’s revenue/margins are sustainable beyond weather; Boone said, “We don’t see a cliff coming or a slowdown happening there.”
  • Min Cho, Texas Capital: Asked about back-half-weighted guidance drivers and risk/upside; Boone said, “I think it’s a timing thing as far as our marine business,” adding, “we’re very confident in the pipeline and backlog.”
  • Min Cho, Texas Capital: Asked for McAmis’ Q1 adjusted EBITDA contribution; Vasquez said, “It contributed positively,” but “nominally,” emphasizing it was “more in opportunity pursuit and building backlog for the future.”
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital: Asked about data center visibility and forward demand; Boone said, “visibility into data centers is pretty minimal until it’s kind of go time,” while adding, “it’s looking really good for the year for data centers for us.”
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital: Asked about capacity constraints and knock-on pricing; Boone said there are “resource challenges, whether it be people, equipment, materials,” and added, “everybody is finding a way.”
  • Liam Burke, B. Riley: Asked whether strong operating cash flow changes deleveraging priorities; Vasquez said, “that’s not our highest priority,” adding, “our priority… would be… investments in key equipment, key people.”
  • Liam Burke, B. Riley: Asked whether M&A remains an allocation option; Boone said, “we’re going to be very disciplined,” and added, “if something comes along that makes good sense… we might be interested in it.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical on guidance discipline and sustainability, including Sano’s question about “no upward revisions,” and Cho’s question on whether Concrete results are “sustainable.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive and conservative, with Boone emphasizing, “we want to underpromise and overdeliver,” and Vasquez repeatedly framing Marine margin pressure as “a timing item.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s prepared tone centered on 2026 guidance and pipeline/backlog timing, the current call added more near-term framing around April awards and maintained conservatism on changing guidance. (CEO, President & Director Boone; CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company moved from outlining initial full-year 2026 guidance in Q4 2025 to stating it would “reaffirm our full year 2026 guidance” in Q1 2026 without changing it. (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • Pipeline commentary increased from “$23 billion” (inclusive of McAmis) in Q4 2025 to a “$24 billion pipeline” in Q1 2026, with Q1 also adding a time distribution: “roughly $8 billion in opportunities for 2026, $8 billion in 2027 and $8 billion in 2028 and beyond.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • Segment reporting transparency described as planned in Q4 2025 became implemented in Q1 2026: “begin reporting 3 segments: Marine, Concrete and Corporate.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)
  • Q&A emphasis shifted from Q4 2025’s backlog timing and margin outlook by segment to Q1 2026’s questions on holding guidance, Marine margin phasing, and Concrete sustainability amid strong execution and weather. (CEO, President & Director Boone; CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)

Risks and Concerns

  • Boone highlighted geopolitical disruption as a market factor: “With the Iran conflict and disruption of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz… These are meaningful drivers of public and private maritime build-outs.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • On policy and cost volatility, Boone said, “The fuel side is something we’re watching,” and added on mitigation, “we tend to build in contingency in our bids… [and] we buy in advance.” (CEO, President & Director Boone)
  • On Marine profitability, Vasquez said the quarter reflected “phasing” and “timing,” and added, “We aren’t seeing any signals that there would be any consistent or persistent margin degradation over time.” (CFO, Executive VP & Treasurer Vasquez)

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as a “solid start to the year” supported by a “$24 billion pipeline,” with Concrete driven heavily by data centers (about 40% of segment revenue) and Marine described as cycling through project timing after large-project ramp downs. Management kept full-year 2026 guidance unchanged, repeatedly describing the approach as conservative, while pointing to backlog of $668 million, April awards “well over $200 million” not yet under contract, and continued confidence that Marine margin pressure is primarily project phasing rather than structural degradation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Peet's and Starbucks plan to grow in China despite the intense coffee pricing war
data.description:
Global coffee chains are adapting their China strategies to navigate an intense price war led by domestic players like Luckin Coffee (LKNCY) and Cotti Coffee, as well as local chains using tech platforms such as Alibaba (BABA), JD.com (JD), and Meituan to scale, according to Nikkei Asia.

JAB Holding's Peet’s Coffee chain, which has been in China since 2017, is now expanding into central cities such as Wuhan, Xi’an, Chengdu, and Zhengzhou, while experimenting with new formats in tourist locations and themed destinations. The company plans to open about 60 new stores this year and is focusing on higher-value positioning, with average spending around 50 yuan per customer. Currently, Peet's operates nearly 300 stores in China, mostly in eastern cities like Shanghai. 

Meanwhile, Starbucks (SBUX), which entered China in 1999 and now has about 8,000 stores, is also shifting strategy. After selling a 60% stake in its China business to Boyu Capital at a roughly $5.2B valuation, the coffee chain is pursuing a "thousand stores, thousand faces" approach by using varied formats like coffee carts to expand into smaller cities and increase coverage to 1,500 districts over three years. 

Looking ahead, analysts point to some signs that the pricing war is easing, with both foreign and domestic players increasingly shifting their focus from aggressive discounting toward differentiated offerings and higher-quality growth.

data.title: WM outlines $2b 2026 buybacks while expecting $30m-$35m annual RNG tax credits through 2029
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Waste Management (WM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Q1 operating EBITDA grew by nearly 6% compared to the first quarter of 2025" and "Q1 free cash flow" was "$920 million," enabling returns of "about $730 million" via "dividends and share repurchases" (CEO James Fish).
  • "In renewable energy, operating EBITDA more than doubled" after "the completion of 7 new renewable natural gas facilities since the first quarter of 2025"; in recycling, despite "pricing for single-stream commodities" down "27%," "operating EBITDA grew by 18%" and the business "processed 9% more volume" (CEO Fish).
  • "Despite a softer volume environment driven largely by winter weather impacts and the absence of last year's wildfire-related volumes," collection and disposal delivered "operating EBITDA growth of more than 6%" with "margin expanding approximately 110 basis points"; "total driver and technician turnover" was "17.2%" and Q1 safety incidents were "our best ever Q1" (President & COO John Morris).
  • "Operating cash flow was $1.5 billion" and "capital expenditures totaled $650 million," with "$61 million" for "sustainability growth investments"; "our leverage ratio" ended at "2.94x" (CFO David Reed).

Outlook

  • "This momentum to start the year... reinforces our confidence in achieving our full year financial guidance" (CEO Fish).
  • "We do expect EBITDA margin to lift nicely... in the second half" while noting "Q2 will be a tough comp for us with the wildfire volumes" (CFO Reed).
  • "We expect to achieve our full year revenue guidance in 2026" and "we expect improvement from seasonality as well as the lapping of a couple of larger low-margin contract losses" (President & COO Morris).
  • "We now expect a full year effective tax rate of approximately 23% in 2026" after IRS clarification on RNG production tax credits (CFO Reed).

Financial results

  • "First quarter free cash flow nearly doubled to $920 million" as "operating cash flow was $1.5 billion" and "capital expenditures totaled $650 million" (CFO Reed).
  • "We returned $385 million to shareholders in dividends" and "repurchasing $344 million"; "we are on track for the $2 billion for the year" in repurchases, "55% to 60% in the second half of the year" (CFO Reed).
  • "Our effective tax rate was approximately 18% in the first quarter"; production tax credit benefit was "approximately $27 million for the 2025 tax year" and "$30 million to $35 million annually from this year through 2029" (CFO Reed).

Q&A

  • Jerry Revich, Wells Fargo: asked for better volume visibility and weather impact; CFO Reed said "Q2 will be a tough comp" but "we do expect EBITDA margin to lift nicely" and CEO Fish said facilities were shut "for as many as 10 days" and volumes look better as wildfire comps roll off.
  • Jerry Revich, Wells Fargo: pressed on Q2 seasonality; President & COO Morris cited wildfire-related revenue of "$85-ish million" and said net of that "we're going to see good margin improvement in Q1 and Q2" but "muted somewhat" by the non-repeat.
  • Unknown analyst: questioned higher corporate expense; CFO Reed said Q1 is "indicative" and "pretty flat throughout the rest of the year at that level in Q1."
  • Unknown analyst: asked about fuel timing; President & COO Morris said billing is "about a month lag" and "not going to be anything material."
  • James Schumm, TD Cowen: asked on transfer volumes; President & COO Morris said it was "probably as much about the Northeast and the weather."
  • Schumm, TD Cowen: asked about Healthcare credits; CEO Fish said credits "peaked in Q4" and "fell off a little bit in Q1 and Q2" and "really reverse" in "Q3 and Q4," adding "DSO down 14 days" and past-due receivables down "by 2/3."
  • Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank: asked recycling pricing; Senior VP Tara Hemmer said March was "about $69 a ton" vs guidance of "$70 a ton" and flagged freight risk from "the Middle East."
  • Alwy, Deutsche Bank: asked cross-selling impact; CEO Fish said, "I don't know that I know the answer to that" but cited "about $27 million" annualized EBITDA benefit.
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: asked pricing strength and CPI lag; CEO Fish said CPI resets have "about a 2-quarter lag" and highlighted resi yield "6.3%" and MSW yield "6.9%."
  • Noah Kaye, Oppenheimer: asked RNG pricing and contracting; Hemmer said production "almost doubled," "80% of our volume is locked in for the year" (up from "60%"), with "3" plants in Q2 and the rest in 2H.
  • Connor Cerniglia, Bernstein: asked AI pricing benefit; CEO Fish said AI cameras let WM "bill them for it" when contamination persists.
  • Rob Wertheimer, Melius: asked 2H Healthcare revenue inflection; CEO Fish said it is "all of the above" across "credits," "price," and "volume," with a "$40 million headwind" mostly in the first half.
  • Sabahat Khan, RBC: asked sustainability guide vs execution risk; Hemmer said the company still expects "$240 million to $250 million" EBITDA benefit, while managing "interconnect delays with the utilities."
  • Konark Gupta, Scotiabank: asked residential shedding duration; President & COO Morris said moderation should show "in the second half of the year" and noted EBITDA in that line from Q1'23 to Q1'26 was up "211%."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: asked corporate expense and long-term SG&A; CFO Reed said SG&A could be "more in that kind of 10% range long term," and CEO Fish said Healthcare SG&A was "down to 17%" from "as high as... 25%."
  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: asked capital deployment and M&A; CFO Reed said "over 90% of our free cash flow" will go to "dividends and share repurchases" and tuck-ins could be "at the high end" of "$100 million to $200 million."
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: asked sustainability margins; Hemmer said recycling is still tracking "roughly 300 basis points of margin expansion" and renewable energy "200 basis points."
  • William Grippin, Barclays: asked EPA RVO impact; Hemmer said prices held at "$2.40 per RIN" and WM can "forward-sell RINs" with 80% volume locked.
  • Kevin Chiang, CIBC: asked recycled plastics and shuttered facility; Hemmer said pricing is "starting to creep back up" but "we're not anticipating any significant benefit" and it "would it change our tune" on shuttered sites.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on volumes, corporate costs, and sustainability timing, including "double-click" requests and cost scrutiny of "the magnitude of the increase in corporate expense" (multiple analysts).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory, repeatedly emphasizing confidence and normalization, including "we expect EBITDA margin to lift nicely" and "we expect to achieve our full year revenue guidance in 2026" (CFO Reed; President & COO Morris).
  • Versus Q4 2025, the prepared tone remained confident on "guidance," but Q&A contained more detailed weather and wildfire-comp discussion than the prior quarter’s broader macro optimism.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 kept the "year of harvest" shareholder-return theme from Q4 2025, with CFO Reed reiterating repurchases are "on track for the $2 billion for the year" versus Q4’s authorization of a larger program discussed for 2026.
  • Weather and wildfire comps became more central in Q1: CEO Fish described "3 feet of snow in Boston" and facility closures, while Q4 referenced weather impacts more briefly and emphasized 2026 wildfire headwinds.
  • Healthcare messaging evolved from Q4’s integration progress to Q1’s near-term inflection framing, with CEO Fish stating "we expect an inflection in revenue growth in the second half of 2026" and credit memo reversals expected in "Q3 and Q4."

Risks and concerns

  • "Softer volume environment" tied to "winter weather impacts" and the "absence of last year's wildfire-related volumes" remained a near-term headwind, with management expecting seasonal improvement and wildfire anniversary easing (President & COO Morris).
  • Sustainability execution risk included "interconnect delays with the utilities that might have been unexpected" even as pricing was tracking better (Senior VP Tara Hemmer).
  • Corporate cost growth remained elevated due to "higher annual incentive compensation" and "increased technology costs," which CFO Reed described as a "normalized kind of rate" going forward.

Final takeaway

WM management framed Q1 as a strong start supported by pricing, margin execution, and higher free cash flow, while positioning the back half of 2026 for margin lift and Healthcare revenue inflection as credit memos unwind and ERP stability improves. Capital allocation stayed centered on heavy shareholder returns, with buybacks ramping later in the year and leverage back inside target range, alongside incremental upside from clarified RNG production tax credits expected to extend through 2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biogen outlines Apellis deal impacts with $120M-$130M 2026 other income headwind and non-GAAP EPS accretion in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Biogen (BIIB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We've had a very strong start to the year" and "the growth products are now generating $850 million in the first quarter, and that's up 12%" (President, CEO & Director Christopher Viehbacher).
  • "We have a PDUFA date of May 24 for the induction subcutaneous" LEQEMBI IQLIK, which "we see ... as an opportunity to facilitate the care pathway, improve patient convenience and improve our competitive profile versus Kisunla" (CEO Viehbacher).
  • On Apellis, "we have the proposed Apellis acquisition" and "we do expect the acquisition to be accretive in 2027" (CEO Viehbacher).
  • "Total revenue was $2.5 billion" and "non-GAAP diluted EPS of $3.57"; "we generated $594 million of free cash flow in the quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Robin Kramer).
  • "New real-world data for LEQEMBI show a strong treatment persistence with nearly 80% of patients remaining on therapy at 18 months and almost 70% at 2 years" (Executive VP & Head of Development Priya Singhal).

Outlook

  • "Our overall belief in the strength of the underlying business is as strong as when we guided in February" and "our expected business outlook and underlying assumptions, including our revenue outlook remain consistent with our prior guidance" (CFO Kramer).
  • "We also expect to incur $145 million or an $0.80 EPS impact of acquired IPR&D charges in the second quarter" including "the TJ Bio transaction for felzartamab rights in China" at "$0.55" and a salanersen milestone at "approximately $0.25" (CFO Kramer).
  • "We expect roughly $600 million of contract manufacturing revenue this year" with "roughly 2/3 coming in the first half of the year" and "we expect Q2 core operating expenses to be roughly consistent with Q1" (CFO Kramer).
  • "We do plan to provide 2026 guidance inclusive of Apellis when we report second quarter results" (CFO Kramer).

Financial Results

  • "Total revenue was $2.5 billion, up 2% year-over-year" with "GAAP diluted EPS of $2.15" and "non-GAAP diluted EPS of $3.57" (CFO Kramer).
  • "LEQEMBI end market revenue was $168 million" and "SKYCLARYS ... global revenue of $151 million" (CFO Kramer).
  • "Non-GAAP R&D expense ... was $480 million" and "Non-GAAP SG&A expense ... was $600 million" (CFO Kramer).
  • "We recorded approximately $34 million of acquired IPR&D in the first quarter of 2026 ... resulting in an approximately $0.20 per share impact" (CFO Kramer).
  • "We generated $594 million of free cash flow" and exited with "$4.7 billion of cash and marketable securities" and "$1.5 billion of net debt" (CFO Kramer).

Q&A

  • Brian Abrahams, RBC: "what kinds of signals" in BIIB080; Executive VP Singhal: "we're looking to see is whether reduction of tau leads to and translates into a clinical benefit" and "we're expecting the data sometime midyear."
  • Lin Tsai, Jefferies: litifilimab filing strategy; Executive VP Singhal: "we think that this would be a package as of now" and if mixed trials "we'd be looking at the totality of data" and "there is precedent for that."
  • Geoffrey Meacham, Citi: BD priorities post-Apellis; CEO Viehbacher: "most of what we're going to be focusing on is early-stage development and research" and on M&A "we would probably be more opportunistic."
  • Michael Yee, UBS: BIIB080 bar for investment; Executive VP Singhal: "we'll be very disciplined" and "we think about our go/no-go criteria well ahead of data readouts"; CEO Viehbacher: "there's just no precedent here" and "the bar doesn't necessarily have to be that high, but ... we do have to see some signs."
  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs: blood-based biomarkers and Kisunla switch; President & Head of North America Alisha Alaimo: "CMS did add that blood-based biomarkers can be used as a confirmation" and physicians are asking "what do we do" as Kisunla patients reach "their 18-month mark."
  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler: SYFOVRE competition and visual acuity; Executive VP Singhal: "the gold standard for assessing efficacy has been lesion growth" and cited "42% ... reduction in lesion growth" plus "5-year long-term data"; CEO Viehbacher: "SYFOVRE has 5-year data" creating "a data moat."
  • Paul Matteis, Stifel: SYFOVRE spend/DTC and prefilled syringe; President Alaimo: "we do know we will be spending money on DTC and TV ads" and "we will find synergies" using Biogen launch capabilities.
  • Mohit Bansal, Wells Fargo: high-dose SPINRAZA switching/persistence; President Alaimo: "20% of my patient base have -- already have start forms"; CFO Kramer: "in Germany ... roughly 20% of the patients converting"; CEO Viehbacher: "this is a franchise that is going to be durable and can grow over time."
  • Evan Seigerman, BMO: SKYCLARYS drivers; President Alaimo: U.S. revenue was impacted by "inventory" and "Q1 has 2 fewer buying weeks"; CFO Kramer: "revenue in the quarter exceeded the U.S. for the first time" ex-U.S.
  • Alexandria Hammond, Wolfe: BIIB080 beyond Alzheimer’s; Executive VP Singhal: "we would be looking for tau reduction" and "it remains high on our list."
  • Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley: felzartamab China rights and opportunity; CEO Viehbacher: AMR is "about probably 11,000 patients" and referenced an IgAN price point of "about $350,000 a year"; Executive VP Singhal: AMR readout is expected "in 2027."
  • Jay Olson, Oppenheimer: LEQEMBI persistence and subcutaneous impact; Executive VP Singhal: "patients and neurologists actually want to continue to stay on therapy" and on subcutaneous initiation "we continue to remain very optimistic."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on de-risking BIIB080 and commercial thresholds (e.g., Michael Yee, UBS: "what type of trend would make sense for you to push forward").
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks emphasizing stability, launches, and Apellis rationale (CEO Viehbacher: "you cannot save your way to prosperity" and "before we had a pipeline coming on a flat business. Now I see a pipeline coming on a growing business").
  • Versus Q4 2025, management language added more transaction-specific financing and integration framing (CFO Kramer: "we plan to fund the transaction with $3.6 billion of cash" and "$2 billion from bank borrowings").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call shifted from Q4’s emphasis on guiding the standalone year and MS erosion to transaction execution and integration timing, with management now saying "we do plan to provide 2026 guidance inclusive of Apellis" after close (CFO Kramer).
  • LEQEMBI subcutaneous induction remained the key near-term catalyst in both quarters (CEO Viehbacher in Q1: "PDUFA date of May 24"; Q4: "PDUFA date of May 24" for IQLIK initiation).
  • Analyst focus broadened from launch cadence and top-line inflection (Q4) to proof-of-concept clarity (BIIB080), competitive positioning (SYFOVRE), and filing strategy (litifilimab) (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Timing of shipments is a big thing at Biogen" and can make products "lumpy by nature" (CEO Viehbacher).
  • On BIIB080, "nobody knows how long you have to reduce the tau for to get a benefit on cognition" and "we're going to be discovering an awful lot in this study" (CEO Viehbacher).
  • On tariffs, "based on potential tariffs as announced to date, we do not expect to see material impact to our business in 2026" while noting it "does not reflect the impact of the pending acquisition of Apellis" (CFO Kramer).

Final Takeaway

Biogen’s management framed Q1 2026 as a strong start driven by a $851M growth-products quarter and improving launch execution, while keeping its core 2026 business assumptions intact and layering in near-term IPR&D-related EPS impacts. The call’s center of gravity was the pending Apellis acquisition—positioned as expanding commercial growth and enhancing nephrology—alongside the May 24 PDUFA for LEQEMBI subcutaneous induction and midyear BIIB080 data, with management repeatedly emphasizing disciplined go/no-go decision-making and execution focus.
